_🎯 Functional Correctness_ | _🟠 Major_ | _⚡ Quick win_

<details>
<summary>🧩 Analysis chain</summary>

🌐 Web query:

`Do JSON Web Tokens (JWT) always use base64url encoding for the header and payload segments, and will the JavaScript atob() function throw on '-' or '_' characters?`

💡 Result:

Yes, JSON Web Tokens (JWT) use Base64url encoding for their header, payload, and signature segments [1][2][3]. According to the official JWT specification (RFC 7519), a JWT is represented as a sequence of URL-safe parts separated by period ('.') characters, where each part contains a Base64url-encoded value [3][4]. The JavaScript atob function will throw a DOMException if it encounters '-' or '_' characters [5][6]. The atob function implements standard Base64 decoding, which uses the '+' and '/' characters for values 62 and 63, respectively [5][7][6]. Because Base64url is a URL-safe variant that replaces '+' with '-' and '/' with '_', and omits the '=' padding characters, passing a Base64url-encoded segment directly to atob results in an "Invalid character" error [5][8][6]. To correctly decode JWT segments in a browser environment using atob, you must first transform the Base64url string back into standard Base64 by: 1. Replacing '-' with '+' and '_' with '/' [5][8][6]. 2. Adding back the necessary '=' padding characters so that the string length is a multiple of 4 [5][8][6]. Alternatively, many developers use dedicated JWT libraries or, in modern environments, alternative decoding methods that handle these conversions automatically [5][6].

Citations:

- 1: https://jwtdecode.app/jwt-header-payload-signature-explained
- 2: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/JSON_Web_Token
- 3: https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc7519?adobe_mc=MCMID%3D19116174769159930413680846041420205648%7CMCORGID%3DA8833BC75245AF9E0A490D4D%40AdobeOrg%7CTS%3D1755870864
- 4: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc7519.html
- 5: https://bytejson.com/tutorials/base64-decode-errors/
- 6: https://developertools.app/articles/how-to-decode-base64-javascript.html
- 7: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/69368185/why-does-atob-fail-in-safari-and-chrome-on-this-base-64-string
- 8: https://dev.to/tommy_worklab/i-built-a-jwt-decoder-and-lost-half-a-day-to-atob-2cok

</details>

**Decode the refresh JWT as base64url** in `src/services/api.js:80-95`
- `atob(token.split('.')[1])` will fail on normal JWT base64url segments, so `exp` falls back to the 24h default `expiresAt` instead of the token’s actual expiry. The current test uses `btoa(...)`, so it doesn’t exercise the URL-safe case.
- Guard `res.data.token` before calling `setAccessToken` and resolving the queue; otherwise a malformed refresh response can write `undefined` into auth state.

<details>
<summary>🧰 Tools</summary>

<details>
<summary>🪛 ast-grep (0.44.0)</summary>

[warning] 91-91: Avoid using the initial state variable in setState
Context: setAccessToken(token, expiresAt)
Note: [CWE-710] Improper Adherence to Coding Standards. Security best practice.

(setstate-same-var)

</details>

</details>

<details>
<summary>🤖 Prompt for AI Agents</summary>

```
Verify each finding against current code. Fix only still-valid issues, skip the
rest with a brief reason, keep changes minimal, and validate.

In `@src/services/api.js` around lines 80 - 95, The refresh-token handling in
api.js needs two fixes: decode the JWT payload as base64url instead of plain
atob(token.split('.')[1]) so exp is read correctly from normal JWTs, and guard
res.data.token before using it. Update the token parsing logic in the refresh
flow to convert the URL-safe segment before JSON parsing, and only call
setAccessToken plus resolve refreshQueue when a valid token exists; otherwise
fail the refresh path cleanly.
```

</details>
